question:
6. If the \( x \)-coordinate \( \bar{x} \) of the center of mass of the area lying between the \( x \)-axis and the curve \( y=f(x),(f(x)>0) \), and between the lines \( x=0 \) and \( x \) \( =a \) is given by
\[
\bar{x}=g(a)
\]
show that
\[
f(x)=A \frac{g^{\prime}(x)}{[x-g(x)]^{2}} e^{\int d x /(x-g(x))}
\]
where \( A \) is a positive constant.
solution:
Solution. By the definition of centroid,
\[
g(x)=\frac{\int_{0}^{x} t f(t) d t}{\int_{0}^{x} f(t) d t}, \quad x \neq 0 .
\]

We confine our attention to positive values of \( x \). Put
\[
F(x)=\int_{0}^{x} f(t) d t ; \quad \text { then } F^{\prime}=f
\]

Write (1) in the form
\[
F(x) g(x)=\int_{0}^{x} t f(t) d t,
\]
and differentiate to get
\[
F^{\prime}(x) g(x)+F(x) g^{\prime}(x)=x f(x)=x F^{\prime}(x)
\]

Thus \( \boldsymbol{F} \) satisfies the linear differential equation
\[
F^{\prime}(x)=\frac{g^{\prime}(x)}{x-g(x)} F(x)
\]

Hence
\[
F(x)=A e^{\psi(x)}
\]
where \( A \) is a positive constant and
\[
\psi(x)=\int^{x} \frac{g^{\prime}(t)}{t-g(t)} d t=\int^{x} \frac{d t}{t-g(t)}-\log (x-g(x))
\]

Thus
\[
F(x)=\frac{A}{x-g(x)} \exp \int^{x} \frac{d t}{t-g(t)}
\]

Substituting (5) into (2) gives us
\[
f(x)=A \frac{g^{\prime}(x)}{(x-g(x))^{2}} \exp \int^{x} \frac{d t}{t-g(t)}
\]
as required.
Under the conditions of the problem, \( x>g(x) \) for all positive \( x \), so the denominator \( x-g(x) \) causes no singularities for positive \( x \). For \( x<0 \), we have \( g(x)>x \), so we must replace \( \log (x-g(x)) \) by \( \log (g(x)-x) \) in (4). Then (6) becomes
\[
f(x)=\frac{-A g^{\prime}(x)}{(x-g(x))^{2}} \exp \int^{x} \frac{d t}{t-g(t)}
\]

However, in this case the constant \( A \) in (3) must be negative so we again have the required form.

descriptive_long.question:
6. If the \( abscissa \)-coordinate \( \bar{abscissa} \) of the center of mass of the area lying between the \( abscissa \)-axis and the curve \( y=curvefn(abscissa),\,(curvefn(abscissa)>0) \), and between the lines \( abscissa=0 \) and \( abscissa =upperbd \) is given by
\[
\bar{abscissa}=centroid(upperbd)
\]
show that
\[
curvefn(abscissa)=constan \frac{centroid^{\prime}(abscissa)}{[abscissa-centroid(abscissa)]^{2}} e^{\int d abscissa /(abscissa-centroid(abscissa))}
\]
where \( constan \) is a positive constant.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. By the definition of centroid,
\[
centroid(abscissa)=\frac{\int_{0}^{abscissa} paramtr\, curvefn(paramtr)\, d paramtr}{\int_{0}^{abscissa} curvefn(paramtr)\, d paramtr}, \quad abscissa \neq 0 .
\]

We confine our attention to positive values of \( abscissa \). Put
\[
antider(abscissa)=\int_{0}^{abscissa} curvefn(paramtr)\, d paramtr ; \quad \text { then } antider^{\prime}=curvefn
\]

Write (1) in the form
\[
antider(abscissa)\, centroid(abscissa)=\int_{0}^{abscissa} paramtr\, curvefn(paramtr)\, d paramtr,
\]
and differentiate to get
\[
antider^{\prime}(abscissa)\, centroid(abscissa)+antider(abscissa)\, centroid^{\prime}(abscissa)=abscissa\, curvefn(abscissa)=abscissa\, antider^{\prime}(abscissa)
\]

Thus \( \mathbf{antider} \) satisfies the linear differential equation
\[
antider^{\prime}(abscissa)=\frac{centroid^{\prime}(abscissa)}{abscissa-centroid(abscissa)}\, antider(abscissa)
\]

Hence
\[
antider(abscissa)=constan\, e^{auxfunc(abscissa)}
\]
where \( constan \) is a positive constant and
\[
auxfunc(abscissa)=\int^{abscissa} \frac{centroid^{\prime}(paramtr)}{paramtr-centroid(paramtr)}\, d paramtr=\int^{abscissa} \frac{d paramtr}{paramtr-centroid(paramtr)}-\log (abscissa-centroid(abscissa))
\]

Thus
\[
antider(abscissa)=\frac{constan}{abscissa-centroid(abscissa)} \exp \int^{abscissa} \frac{d paramtr}{paramtr-centroid(paramtr)}
\]

Substituting (5) into (2) gives us
\[
curvefn(abscissa)=constan \frac{centroid^{\prime}(abscissa)}{(abscissa-centroid(abscissa))^{2}} \exp \int^{abscissa} \frac{d paramtr}{paramtr-centroid(paramtr)}
\]
as required.

Under the conditions of the problem, \( abscissa>centroid(abscissa) \) for all positive \( abscissa \), so the denominator \( abscissa-centroid(abscissa) \) causes no singularities for positive \( abscissa \). For \( abscissa<0 \), we have \( centroid(abscissa)>abscissa \), so we must replace \( \log (abscissa-centroid(abscissa)) \) by \( \log (centroid(abscissa)-abscissa) \) in (4). Then (6) becomes
\[
curvefn(abscissa)=\frac{-constan\, centroid^{\prime}(abscissa)}{(abscissa-centroid(abscissa))^{2}} \exp \int^{abscissa} \frac{d paramtr}{paramtr-centroid(paramtr)}
\]

However, in this case the constant \( constan \) in (3) must be negative so we again have the required form.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
6. If the \( pebblestone \)-coordinate \( \bar{pebblestone} \) of the center of mass of the area lying between the x-axis and the curve \( y=windchimes(pebblestone),(windchimes(pebblestone)>0) \), and between the lines \( pebblestone=0 \) and \( pebblestone \) \( =raincloud \) is given by
\[
\bar{pebblestone}=lighthouse(raincloud)
\]
show that
\[
windchimes(pebblestone)=moonflower \frac{lighthouse^{\prime}(pebblestone)}{[pebblestone-lighthouse(pebblestone)]^{2}} e^{\int d pebblestone /(pebblestone-lighthouse(pebblestone))}
\]
where \( moonflower \) is a positive constant.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. By the definition of centroid,
\[
lighthouse(pebblestone)=\frac{\int_{0}^{pebblestone} marigold\, windchimes(marigold) d marigold}{\int_{0}^{pebblestone} windchimes(marigold) d marigold}, \quad pebblestone \neq 0 .
\]

We confine our attention to positive values of \( pebblestone \). Put
\[
buttercup(pebblestone)=\int_{0}^{pebblestone} windchimes(marigold) d marigold ; \quad \text { then } buttercup^{\prime}=windchimes
\]

Write (1) in the form
\[
buttercup(pebblestone) \, lighthouse(pebblestone)=\int_{0}^{pebblestone} marigold \, windchimes(marigold) d marigold,
\]
and differentiate to get
\[
buttercup^{\prime}(pebblestone) \, lighthouse(pebblestone)+buttercup(pebblestone) \, lighthouse^{\prime}(pebblestone)=pebblestone \, windchimes(pebblestone)=pebblestone \, buttercup^{\prime}(pebblestone)
\]

Thus \( \boldsymbol{buttercup} \) satisfies the linear differential equation
\[
buttercup^{\prime}(pebblestone)=\frac{lighthouse^{\prime}(pebblestone)}{pebblestone-lighthouse(pebblestone)} \, buttercup(pebblestone)
\]

Hence
\[
buttercup(pebblestone)=moonflower \, e^{tumbleweed(pebblestone)}
\]
where \( moonflower \) is a positive constant and
\[
tumbleweed(pebblestone)=\int^{pebblestone} \frac{lighthouse^{\prime}(marigold)}{marigold-lighthouse(marigold)} d marigold=\int^{pebblestone} \frac{d marigold}{marigold-lighthouse(marigold)}-\log (pebblestone-lighthouse(pebblestone))
\]

Thus
\[
buttercup(pebblestone)=\frac{moonflower}{pebblestone-lighthouse(pebblestone)} \exp \int^{pebblestone} \frac{d marigold}{marigold-lighthouse(marigold)}
\]

Substituting (5) into (2) gives us
\[
windchimes(pebblestone)=moonflower \frac{lighthouse^{\prime}(pebblestone)}{(pebblestone-lighthouse(pebblestone))^{2}} \exp \int^{pebblestone} \frac{d marigold}{marigold-lighthouse(marigold)}
\]
as required.
Under the conditions of the problem, \( pebblestone>lighthouse(pebblestone) \) for all positive \( pebblestone \), so the denominator \( pebblestone-lighthouse(pebblestone) \) causes no singularities for positive \( pebblestone \). For \( pebblestone<0 \), we have \( lighthouse(pebblestone)>pebblestone \), so we must replace \( \log (pebblestone-lighthouse(pebblestone)) \) by \( \log (lighthouse(pebblestone)-pebblestone) \) in (4). Then (6) becomes
\[
windchimes(pebblestone)=\frac{-moonflower \, lighthouse^{\prime}(pebblestone)}{(pebblestone-lighthouse(pebblestone))^{2}} \exp \int^{pebblestone} \frac{d marigold}{marigold-lighthouse(marigold)}
\]

However, in this case the constant \( moonflower \) in (3) must be negative so we again have the required form.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
6. If the \( fixedvalue \)-coordinate \( \bar{fixedvalue} \) of the center of mass of the area lying between the \( fixedvalue \)-axis and the curve \( y=constant(fixedvalue),(constant(fixedvalue)>0) \), and between the lines \( fixedvalue=0 \) and \( fixedvalue \) \( =variable \) is given by
\[
\bar{fixedvalue}=randomness(variable)
\]
show that
\[
constant(fixedvalue)=negative \frac{randomness^{\prime}(fixedvalue)}{[fixedvalue-randomness(fixedvalue)]^{2}} e^{\int d fixedvalue /(fixedvalue-randomness(fixedvalue))}
\]
where \( negative \) is a positive constant.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. By the definition of centroid,
\[
randomness(fixedvalue)=\frac{\int_{0}^{fixedvalue} timeless constant(timeless) d timeless}{\int_{0}^{fixedvalue} constant(timeless) d timeless}, \quad fixedvalue \neq 0 .
\]

We confine our attention to positive values of \( fixedvalue \). Put
\[
derivative(fixedvalue)=\int_{0}^{fixedvalue} constant(timeless) d timeless ; \quad \text { then } derivative^{\prime}=constant
\]

Write (1) in the form
\[
derivative(fixedvalue) randomness(fixedvalue)=\int_{0}^{fixedvalue} timeless constant(timeless) d timeless,
\]
and differentiate to get
\[
derivative^{\prime}(fixedvalue) randomness(fixedvalue)+derivative(fixedvalue) randomness^{\prime}(fixedvalue)=fixedvalue constant(fixedvalue)=fixedvalue derivative^{\prime}(fixedvalue)
\]

Thus \( \boldsymbol{derivative} \) satisfies the linear differential equation
\[
derivative^{\prime}(fixedvalue)=\frac{randomness^{\prime}(fixedvalue)}{fixedvalue-randomness(fixedvalue)} derivative(fixedvalue)
\]

Hence
\[
derivative(fixedvalue)=negative e^{ignorance(fixedvalue)}
\]
where \( negative \) is a positive constant and
\[
ignorance(fixedvalue)=\int^{fixedvalue} \frac{randomness^{\prime}(timeless)}{timeless-randomness(timeless)} d timeless=\int^{fixedvalue} \frac{d timeless}{timeless-randomness(timeless)}-\log (fixedvalue-randomness(fixedvalue))
\]

Thus
\[
derivative(fixedvalue)=\frac{negative}{fixedvalue-randomness(fixedvalue)} \exp \int^{fixedvalue} \frac{d timeless}{timeless-randomness(timeless)}
\]

Substituting (5) into (2) gives us
\[
constant(fixedvalue)=negative \frac{randomness^{\prime}(fixedvalue)}{(fixedvalue-randomness(fixedvalue))^{2}} \exp \int^{fixedvalue} \frac{d timeless}{timeless-randomness(timeless)}
\]
as required.
Under the conditions of the problem, \( fixedvalue>randomness(fixedvalue) \) for all positive \( fixedvalue \), so the denominator \( fixedvalue-randomness(fixedvalue) \) causes no singularities for positive \( fixedvalue \). For \( fixedvalue<0 \), we have \( randomness(fixedvalue)>fixedvalue \), so we must replace \( \log (fixedvalue-randomness(fixedvalue)) \) by \( \log (randomness(fixedvalue)-fixedvalue) \) in (4). Then (6) becomes
\[
constant(fixedvalue)=\frac{-negative randomness^{\prime}(fixedvalue)}{(fixedvalue-randomness(fixedvalue))^{2}} \exp \int^{fixedvalue} \frac{d timeless}{timeless-randomness(timeless)}
\]

However, in this case the constant \( negative \) in (3) must be negative so we again have the required form.

garbled_string.question:
6. If the \( qzxwvtnp \)-coordinate \( \bar{qzxwvtnp} \) of the center of mass of the area lying between the \( qzxwvtnp \)-axis and the curve \( y=rtyhqsld(qzxwvtnp),(rtyhqsld(qzxwvtnp)>0) \), and between the lines \( qzxwvtnp=0 \) and \( qzxwvtnp \) \( =scvmbnld \) is given by
\[
\bar{qzxwvtnp}=plmnxbcq(scvmbnld)
\]
show that
\[
rtyhqsld(qzxwvtnp)=wqlkzjtr \frac{plmnxbcq^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp)}{[qzxwvtnp-plmnxbcq(qzxwvtnp)]^{2}} e^{\int d qzxwvtnp /(qzxwvtnp-plmnxbcq(qzxwvtnp))}
\]
where \( wqlkzjtr \) is a positive constant.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. By the definition of centroid,
\[
plmnxbcq(qzxwvtnp)=\frac{\int_{0}^{qzxwvtnp} hjgrksla \, rtyhqsld(hjgrksla) d hjgrksla}{\int_{0}^{qzxwvtnp} rtyhqsld(hjgrksla) d hjgrksla}, \quad qzxwvtnp \neq 0 .
\]

We confine our attention to positive values of \( qzxwvtnp \). Put
\[
bdmvxpla(qzxwvtnp)=\int_{0}^{qzxwvtnp} rtyhqsld(hjgrksla) d hjgrksla ; \quad \text { then } bdmvxpla^{\prime}=rtyhqsld
\]

Write (1) in the form
\[
bdmvxpla(qzxwvtnp) plmnxbcq(qzxwvtnp)=\int_{0}^{qzxwvtnp} hjgrksla \, rtyhqsld(hjgrksla) d hjgrksla,
\]
and differentiate to get
\[
bdmvxpla^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp) plmnxbcq(qzxwvtnp)+bdmvxpla(qzxwvtnp) plmnxbcq^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp \, rtyhqsld(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp \, bdmvxpla^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp)
\]

Thus \( \boldsymbol{bdmvxpla} \) satisfies the linear differential equation
\[
bdmvxpla^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp)=\frac{plmnxbcq^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp)}{qzxwvtnp - plmnxbcq(qzxwvtnp)} bdmvxpla(qzxwvtnp)
\]

Hence
\[
bdmvxpla(qzxwvtnp)=wqlkzjtr e^{zpkhmytr(qzxwvtnp)}
\]
where \( wqlkzjtr \) is a positive constant and
\[
zpkhmytr(qzxwvtnp)=\int^{qzxwvtnp} \frac{plmnxbcq^{\prime}(hjgrksla)}{hjgrksla-plmnxbcq(hjgrksla)} d hjgrksla=\int^{qzxwvtnp} \frac{d hjgrksla}{hjgrksla-plmnxbcq(hjgrksla)}-\log (qzxwvtnp-plmnxbcq(qzxwvtnp))
\]

Thus
\[
bdmvxpla(qzxwvtnp)=\frac{wqlkzjtr}{qzxwvtnp-plmnxbcq(qzxwvtnp)} \exp \int^{qzxwvtnp} \frac{d hjgrksla}{hjgrksla-plmnxbcq(hjgrksla)}
\]

Substituting (5) into (2) gives us
\[
rtyhqsld(qzxwvtnp)=wqlkzjtr \frac{plmnxbcq^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp)}{(qzxwvtnp-plmnxbcq(qzxwvtnp))^{2}} \exp \int^{qzxwvtnp} \frac{d hjgrksla}{hjgrksla-plmnxbcq(hjgrksla)}
\]
as required.

Under the conditions of the problem, \( qzxwvtnp>plmnxbcq(qzxwvtnp) \) for all positive \( qzxwvtnp \), so the denominator \( qzxwvtnp-plmnxbcq(qzxwvtnp) \) causes no singularities for positive \( qzxwvtnp \). For \( qzxwvtnp<0 \), we have \( plmnxbcq(qzxwvtnp)>qzxwvtnp \), so we must replace \( \log (qzxwvtnp-plmnxbcq(qzxwvtnp)) \) by \( \log (plmnxbcq(qzxwvtnp)-qzxwvtnp) \) in (4). Then (6) becomes
\[
rtyhqsld(qzxwvtnp)=\frac{-wqlkzjtr \, plmnxbcq^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp)}{(qzxwvtnp-plmnxbcq(qzxwvtnp))^{2}} \exp \int^{qzxwvtnp} \frac{d hjgrksla}{hjgrksla-plmnxbcq(hjgrksla)}
\]

However, in this case the constant \( wqlkzjtr \) in (3) must be negative so we again have the required form.

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer $n\ge 3$ and a real number $b$.  
Let  

\[
u,\;v:(b,\infty)\longrightarrow(0,\infty)
\]

be $C^{1}$-functions satisfying  

\[
0<v(x)<u(x)\qquad\forall x>b .
\]

For every $a>b$ form the hollow $n$-dimensional body  

\[
S(a)=\Bigl\{(x,y)\in\mathbb R\times\mathbb R^{\,n-1}:
               \;b\le x\le a,\;
               v(x)\le |y|\le u(x)\Bigr\},
\]

obtained by revolving, about the $x$-axis, the graphs $r=u(x)$ (outer boundary) and  
$r=v(x)$ (inner cavity).  
The material has uniform density $\rho\equiv1$.

Introduce  

\[
\begin{aligned}
&V(a)=\text{volume of }S(a),\\[2pt]
&g(a)=\text{$x$-coordinate of the centroid of }S(a),\\[2pt]
&J(a)=\int_{S(a)}|y|^{2}\,dV\quad\text{(polar moment of inertia about the $x$-axis)}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Assume that, for every $x>b$,  

\[
x>g(x),\qquad g'(x)>0 .
\]

Furthermore suppose  

\[
\begin{cases}
\text{\rm(i)} & g\in C^{1}(b,\infty),\\[4pt]
\text{\rm(ii)}& \displaystyle\int_{b}^{x}\frac{dt}{\,t-g(t)\,}\quad\text{converges for every }x>b,\\[10pt]
\text{\rm(iii)}& h(a):=\dfrac{J(a)}{V(a)}\in C^{1}(b,\infty)\ \text{and } h(a)\neq0,\\[10pt]
\text{\rm(iv)} & V'(x)>0,\;J'(x)>0\quad\forall x>b .
\end{cases}
\]

Throughout set $N:=n-1$ and denote by  

\[
\omega_{N}:=\frac{\pi^{N/2}}{\Gamma\!\bigl(1+N/2\bigr)}
\]

the volume of the $N$-dimensional unit ball.

A.  (Reconstruction of the cross-sectional area)  
Show that a positive constant  

\[
K=K(n,b,u,v)>0
\]

exists such that, for every $x>b$,

\[
\boxed{
u(x)^{N}-v(x)^{N}=K\,
      g'(x)\,[x-g(x)]^{-2}\,
      \exp\!\Bigl(\int_{b}^{x}\frac{dt}{\,t-g(t)\,}\Bigr)
}\tag{$\star$}
\]

B.  (First and second sectional moments)  
Define  

\[
\Delta(x):=u(x)^{N}-v(x)^{N},\qquad
\Sigma(x):=u(x)^{N+2}-v(x)^{N+2}.
\]

Prove that  

\[
\boxed{\Delta(x)=\frac{V'(x)}{\omega_{N}}}\tag{B$_1$}\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\boxed{\Sigma(x)=\frac{N+2}{N\,\omega_{N}}\,J'(x)}\tag{B$_2$}.
\]

Using $J(x)=h(x)\,V(x)$ deduce  

\[
\boxed{
\Sigma(x)=\frac{N+2}{N}\,
           \Bigl[\,h'(x)\,\frac{x-g(x)}{g'(x)}+h(x)\Bigr]\,
           \Delta(x)
}\tag{$\star\star$}
\]

C.  (Explicit formulae when $n=3$)  
Fix $n=3$ (hence $N=2$).  Show that  

\[
\begin{aligned}
u(x)^{2}+v(x)^{2}&=\frac{\Sigma(x)}{\Delta(x)},\tag{C$_1$}\\
u(x)^{2}-v(x)^{2}&=\Delta(x).\tag{C$_2$}
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence prove  

\[
\boxed{
u(x)=\sqrt{\dfrac{\Sigma(x)+\Delta(x)^{2}}{2\Delta(x)}},\qquad
v(x)=\sqrt{\dfrac{\Sigma(x)-\Delta(x)^{2}}{2\Delta(x)}}
}\tag{C$_3$}
\]

where  

\[
\begin{aligned}
\Delta(x)&=K\,
           g'(x)\,[x-g(x)]^{-2}\,
           \exp\!\Bigl(\int_{b}^{x}\frac{dt}{\,t-g(t)\,}\Bigr),\tag{C$_4$}\\[6pt]
\Sigma(x)&=2\Bigl[
              h'(x)\,\frac{x-g(x)}{g'(x)}+h(x)
           \Bigr]\,
           \Delta(x).\tag{C$_5$}
\end{aligned}
\]

Finally verify that $\Sigma(x)\pm\Delta(x)^{2}>0$ for all $x>b$, so that $u(x)>v(x)>0$.



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
\textbf{Step 0.  Cross-sectional geometry.}  
Let $N:=n-1$.  For each $x>b$ define the annular cross-section  

\[
A(x)=\{\,y\in\mathbb R^{N}: v(x)\le|y|\le u(x)\}.
\]

In polar coordinates  

\[
\lvert A(x)\rvert=\omega_{N}\bigl(u(x)^{N}-v(x)^{N}\bigr),\tag{S0.1}
\]

\[
I_{2}(x):=\int_{A(x)}|y|^{2}\,dA=\frac{N\,\omega_{N}}{N+2}\bigl(u(x)^{N+2}-v(x)^{N+2}\bigr).\tag{S0.2}
\]

Set  

\[
V(x):=\int_{b}^{x}\lvert A(t)\rvert\,dt,\qquad  
J(x):=\int_{b}^{x}I_{2}(t)\,dt,
\]

so that $V'(x)=\lvert A(x)\rvert$ and $J'(x)=I_{2}(x)$.

\medskip
\textbf{Step 1.  A differential equation for $V$.}  
By definition of the centroid,

\[
g(x)\,V(x)=\int_{b}^{x}t\,\lvert A(t)\rvert\,dt.\tag{S1.1}
\]

Differentiating,

\[
\lvert A(x)\rvert\,g(x)+V(x)\,g'(x)=x\,\lvert A(x)\rvert.\tag{S1.2}
\]

Because $\lvert A\rvert=V'$, division by $V$ gives  

\[
\frac{V'(x)}{V(x)}=\frac{g'(x)}{x-g(x)}.\tag{S1.3}
\]

Assumption (ii) guarantees that the integrating factor is well-defined, yielding  

\[
V(x)=A_{0}\,
      \frac{\exp\!\bigl(\displaystyle\int_{b}^{x}\frac{dt}{t-g(t)}\bigr)}
           {x-g(x)},\qquad A_{0}>0.\tag{S1.4}
\]

\textbf{Strict positivity of $A_{0}$.}  
Because $x>g(x)$ and $V'(x)=\lvert A(x)\rvert>0$, formula (S1.3) implies $V>0$ on $(b,\infty)$, hence $A_{0}>0$.

Differentiating (S1.4),

\[
\lvert A(x)\rvert=V'(x)=A_{0}\,
      g'(x)\,[x-g(x)]^{-2}\,
      \exp\!\Bigl(\int_{b}^{x}\tfrac{dt}{t-g(t)}\Bigr).\tag{S1.5}
\]

Insert (S0.1) into (S1.5) and set $K:=A_{0}/\omega_{N}>0$; then  

\[
u(x)^{N}-v(x)^{N}=K\,
      g'(x)\,[x-g(x)]^{-2}\,
      \exp\!\Bigl(\int_{b}^{x}\tfrac{dt}{t-g(t)}\Bigr),
\]

which is exactly ($\star$).

\medskip
\textbf{Step 2.  Proof of (B$_1$) and (B$_2$).}  
Formula (S0.1) gives $\Delta(x)=\lvert A(x)\rvert/\omega_{N}=V'(x)/\omega_{N}$, proving (B$_1$).  
Similarly, (S0.2) implies  

\[
J'(x)=I_{2}(x)=\frac{N\,\omega_{N}}{N+2}\,\Sigma(x)
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Sigma(x)=\frac{N+2}{N\,\omega_{N}}\,J'(x),
\]

which is (B$_2$).

\medskip
\textbf{Step 3.  Eliminating $J$ and $V$.}  
With $J(x)=h(x)\,V(x)$,

\[
J'(x)=h'(x)\,V(x)+h(x)\,V'(x).\tag{S3.1}
\]

Insert (S3.1) into (B$_2$) and use (B$_1$):

\[
\Sigma(x)=\frac{N+2}{N}\Bigl[h'(x)\,\frac{V(x)}{V'(x)}+h(x)\Bigr]\Delta(x).
\]

Finally, (S1.3) gives $V(x)/V'(x)=(x-g(x))/g'(x)$; hence ($\star\star$) follows.

\medskip
\textbf{Step 4.  Specialisation to $n=3$ ($N=2$).}  
With $N=2$, $\omega_{2}=\pi$ and $(N+2)/N=2$.  
Equation ($\star$) becomes  

\[
\Delta(x)=K\,
          g'(x)\,[x-g(x)]^{-2}\,
          \exp\!\Bigl(\int_{b}^{x}\tfrac{dt}{t-g(t)}\Bigr),\tag{C4}
\]

while ($\star\star$) reads  

\[
\Sigma(x)=2\Bigl[
              h'(x)\,\frac{x-g(x)}{g'(x)}+h(x)
           \Bigr]\,\Delta(x).\tag{C5}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{Step 5.  Extracting $u$ and $v$.}  
For $N=2$ the algebraic identity  

\[
u^{4}-v^{4}=(u^{2}-v^{2})(u^{2}+v^{2})\tag{S5.1}
\]

holds, i.e.\ $\Sigma(x)=\Delta(x)\bigl(u(x)^{2}+v(x)^{2}\bigr)$.  Consequently

\[
u(x)^{2}+v(x)^{2}=\frac{\Sigma(x)}{\Delta(x)},\qquad
u(x)^{2}-v(x)^{2}=\Delta(x),\tag{S5.2}
\]

which are (C$_1$)-(C$_2$).  Solving the linear system (add and subtract) yields  

\[
u(x)^{2}=\frac{\Sigma(x)+\Delta(x)^{2}}{2\Delta(x)},\qquad
v(x)^{2}=\frac{\Sigma(x)-\Delta(x)^{2}}{2\Delta(x)},\tag{S5.3}
\]

namely (C$_3$).

\smallskip
\textbf{Positivity of the radicands.}  
Because $u>v>0$ we have $\Delta>0$.  Moreover, $\Sigma=u^{4}-v^{4}>\Delta^{2}$, hence  
$\Sigma\pm\Delta^{2} >0$, so the square roots in (C$_3$) are real and positive. With $\Sigma>\Delta^{2}$ the second square root is smaller, giving $u(x)>v(x)>0$ for all $x>b$.

\smallskip
\textbf{Dependence on prescribed data.}  
The right-hand sides of (C$_3$)-(C$_5$) involve only $g,h$, the constant $K$, and universal constants.  Thus the radial profiles $u$ and $v$ are uniquely determined by the one-dimensional data $g$ and $h$.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer $n\ge 3$ and a real number $b$.  
Let  

\[
u,\;v:(b,\infty)\longrightarrow(0,\infty)
\]

be $C^{1}$-functions satisfying  

\[
0<v(x)<u(x)\qquad\forall x>b .
\]

For every $a>b$ form the hollow $n$-dimensional body  

\[
S(a)=\Bigl\{(x,y)\in\mathbb R\times\mathbb R^{\,n-1}:
               \;b\le x\le a,\;
               v(x)\le |y|\le u(x)\Bigr\},
\]

obtained by revolving, about the $x$-axis, the graphs $r=u(x)$ (outer boundary) and  
$r=v(x)$ (inner cavity).  
The material has uniform density $\rho\equiv1$.

Introduce  

\[
\begin{aligned}
&V(a)=\text{volume of }S(a),\\[2pt]
&g(a)=\text{$x$-coordinate of the centroid of }S(a),\\[2pt]
&J(a)=\int_{S(a)}|y|^{2}\,dV\quad\text{(polar moment of inertia about the $x$-axis)}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Assume that, for every $x>b$,  

\[
x>g(x),\qquad g'(x)>0 .
\]

Furthermore suppose  

\[
\begin{cases}
\text{\rm(i)} & g\in C^{1}(b,\infty),\\[4pt]
\text{\rm(ii)}& \displaystyle\int_{b}^{x}\frac{dt}{\,t-g(t)\,}\quad\text{converges for every }x>b,\\[10pt]
\text{\rm(iii)}& h(a):=\dfrac{J(a)}{V(a)}\in C^{1}(b,\infty)\ \text{and } h(a)\neq0,\\[10pt]
\text{\rm(iv)} & V'(x)>0,\;J'(x)>0\quad\forall x>b .
\end{cases}
\]

Throughout set $N:=n-1$ and denote by  

\[
\omega_{N}:=\frac{\pi^{N/2}}{\Gamma\!\bigl(1+N/2\bigr)}
\]

the volume of the $N$-dimensional unit ball.

A.  (Reconstruction of the cross-sectional area)  
Show that a positive constant  

\[
K=K(n,b,u,v)>0
\]

exists such that, for every $x>b$,

\[
\boxed{
u(x)^{N}-v(x)^{N}=K\,
      g'(x)\,[x-g(x)]^{-2}\,
      \exp\!\Bigl(\int_{b}^{x}\frac{dt}{\,t-g(t)\,}\Bigr)
}\tag{$\star$}
\]

B.  (First and second sectional moments)  
Define  

\[
\Delta(x):=u(x)^{N}-v(x)^{N},\qquad
\Sigma(x):=u(x)^{N+2}-v(x)^{N+2}.
\]

Prove that  

\[
\boxed{\Delta(x)=\frac{V'(x)}{\omega_{N}}}\tag{B$_1$}\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\boxed{\Sigma(x)=\frac{N+2}{N\,\omega_{N}}\,J'(x)}\tag{B$_2$}.
\]

Using $J(x)=h(x)\,V(x)$ deduce  

\[
\boxed{
\Sigma(x)=\frac{N+2}{N}\,
           \Bigl[\,h'(x)\,\frac{x-g(x)}{g'(x)}+h(x)\Bigr]\,
           \Delta(x)
}\tag{$\star\star$}
\]

C.  (Explicit formulae when $n=3$)  
Fix $n=3$ (hence $N=2$).  Show that  

\[
\begin{aligned}
u(x)^{2}+v(x)^{2}&=\frac{\Sigma(x)}{\Delta(x)},\tag{C$_1$}\\
u(x)^{2}-v(x)^{2}&=\Delta(x).\tag{C$_2$}
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence prove  

\[
\boxed{
u(x)=\sqrt{\dfrac{\Sigma(x)+\Delta(x)^{2}}{2\Delta(x)}},\qquad
v(x)=\sqrt{\dfrac{\Sigma(x)-\Delta(x)^{2}}{2\Delta(x)}}
}\tag{C$_3$}
\]

where  

\[
\begin{aligned}
\Delta(x)&=K\,
           g'(x)\,[x-g(x)]^{-2}\,
           \exp\!\Bigl(\int_{b}^{x}\frac{dt}{\,t-g(t)\,}\Bigr),\tag{C$_4$}\\[6pt]
\Sigma(x)&=2\Bigl[
              h'(x)\,\frac{x-g(x)}{g'(x)}+h(x)
           \Bigr]\,
           \Delta(x).\tag{C$_5$}
\end{aligned}
\]

Finally verify that $\Sigma(x)\pm\Delta(x)^{2}>0$ for all $x>b$, so that $u(x)>v(x)>0$.



%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
original_kernel_variant.solution:
\textbf{Step 0.  Cross-sectional geometry.}  
Let $N:=n-1$.  For each $x>b$ define the annular cross-section  

\[
A(x)=\{\,y\in\mathbb R^{N}: v(x)\le|y|\le u(x)\}.
\]

In polar coordinates  

\[
\lvert A(x)\rvert=\omega_{N}\bigl(u(x)^{N}-v(x)^{N}\bigr),\tag{S0.1}
\]

\[
I_{2}(x):=\int_{A(x)}|y|^{2}\,dA=\frac{N\,\omega_{N}}{N+2}\bigl(u(x)^{N+2}-v(x)^{N+2}\bigr).\tag{S0.2}
\]

Set  

\[
V(x):=\int_{b}^{x}\lvert A(t)\rvert\,dt,\qquad  
J(x):=\int_{b}^{x}I_{2}(t)\,dt,
\]

so that $V'(x)=\lvert A(x)\rvert$ and $J'(x)=I_{2}(x)$.

\medskip
\textbf{Step 1.  A differential equation for $V$.}  
By definition of the centroid,

\[
g(x)\,V(x)=\int_{b}^{x}t\,\lvert A(t)\rvert\,dt.\tag{S1.1}
\]

Differentiating,

\[
\lvert A(x)\rvert\,g(x)+V(x)\,g'(x)=x\,\lvert A(x)\rvert.\tag{S1.2}
\]

Because $\lvert A\rvert=V'$, division by $V$ gives  

\[
\frac{V'(x)}{V(x)}=\frac{g'(x)}{x-g(x)}.\tag{S1.3}
\]

Assumption (ii) guarantees that the integrating factor is well-defined, yielding  

\[
V(x)=A_{0}\,
      \frac{\exp\!\bigl(\displaystyle\int_{b}^{x}\frac{dt}{t-g(t)}\bigr)}
           {x-g(x)},\qquad A_{0}>0.\tag{S1.4}
\]

\textbf{Strict positivity of $A_{0}$.}  
Because $x>g(x)$ and $V'(x)=\lvert A(x)\rvert>0$, formula (S1.3) implies $V>0$ on $(b,\infty)$, hence $A_{0}>0$.

Differentiating (S1.4),

\[
\lvert A(x)\rvert=V'(x)=A_{0}\,
      g'(x)\,[x-g(x)]^{-2}\,
      \exp\!\Bigl(\int_{b}^{x}\tfrac{dt}{t-g(t)}\Bigr).\tag{S1.5}
\]

Insert (S0.1) into (S1.5) and set $K:=A_{0}/\omega_{N}>0$; then  

\[
u(x)^{N}-v(x)^{N}=K\,
      g'(x)\,[x-g(x)]^{-2}\,
      \exp\!\Bigl(\int_{b}^{x}\tfrac{dt}{t-g(t)}\Bigr),
\]

which is exactly ($\star$).

\medskip
\textbf{Step 2.  Proof of (B$_1$) and (B$_2$).}  
Formula (S0.1) gives $\Delta(x)=\lvert A(x)\rvert/\omega_{N}=V'(x)/\omega_{N}$, proving (B$_1$).  
Similarly, (S0.2) implies  

\[
J'(x)=I_{2}(x)=\frac{N\,\omega_{N}}{N+2}\,\Sigma(x)
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Sigma(x)=\frac{N+2}{N\,\omega_{N}}\,J'(x),
\]

which is (B$_2$).

\medskip
\textbf{Step 3.  Eliminating $J$ and $V$.}  
With $J(x)=h(x)\,V(x)$,

\[
J'(x)=h'(x)\,V(x)+h(x)\,V'(x).\tag{S3.1}
\]

Insert (S3.1) into (B$_2$) and use (B$_1$):

\[
\Sigma(x)=\frac{N+2}{N}\Bigl[h'(x)\,\frac{V(x)}{V'(x)}+h(x)\Bigr]\Delta(x).
\]

Finally, (S1.3) gives $V(x)/V'(x)=(x-g(x))/g'(x)$; hence ($\star\star$) follows.

\medskip
\textbf{Step 4.  Specialisation to $n=3$ ($N=2$).}  
With $N=2$, $\omega_{2}=\pi$ and $(N+2)/N=2$.  
Equation ($\star$) becomes  

\[
\Delta(x)=K\,
          g'(x)\,[x-g(x)]^{-2}\,
          \exp\!\Bigl(\int_{b}^{x}\tfrac{dt}{t-g(t)}\Bigr),\tag{C4}
\]

while ($\star\star$) reads  

\[
\Sigma(x)=2\Bigl[
              h'(x)\,\frac{x-g(x)}{g'(x)}+h(x)
           \Bigr]\,\Delta(x).\tag{C5}
\]

\medskip
\textbf{Step 5.  Extracting $u$ and $v$.}  
For $N=2$ the algebraic identity  

\[
u^{4}-v^{4}=(u^{2}-v^{2})(u^{2}+v^{2})\tag{S5.1}
\]

holds, i.e.\ $\Sigma(x)=\Delta(x)\bigl(u(x)^{2}+v(x)^{2}\bigr)$.  Consequently

\[
u(x)^{2}+v(x)^{2}=\frac{\Sigma(x)}{\Delta(x)},\qquad
u(x)^{2}-v(x)^{2}=\Delta(x),\tag{S5.2}
\]

which are (C$_1$)-(C$_2$).  Solving the linear system (add and subtract) yields  

\[
u(x)^{2}=\frac{\Sigma(x)+\Delta(x)^{2}}{2\Delta(x)},\qquad
v(x)^{2}=\frac{\Sigma(x)-\Delta(x)^{2}}{2\Delta(x)},\tag{S5.3}
\]

namely (C$_3$).

\smallskip
\textbf{Positivity of the radicands.}  
Because $u>v>0$ we have $\Delta>0$.  Moreover, $\Sigma=u^{4}-v^{4}>\Delta^{2}$, hence  
$\Sigma\pm\Delta^{2} >0$, so the square roots in (C$_3$) are real and positive. With $\Sigma>\Delta^{2}$ the second square root is smaller, giving $u(x)>v(x)>0$ for all $x>b$.

\smallskip
\textbf{Dependence on prescribed data.}  
The right-hand sides of (C$_3$)-(C$_5$) involve only $g,h$, the constant $K$, and universal constants.  Thus the radial profiles $u$ and $v$ are uniquely determined by the one-dimensional data $g$ and $h$.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
